title: Cost Monitoring & Budgets
concept: Tracking per-request cost and setting alerts before a pipeline change silently spikes the bill.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about Model Routing. What is the core idea behind this technique?

options.text: Sending all queries to the largest model to ensure maximum quality.
Routing easy questions to a cheap model and hard questions to a costly model.
Caching every single query indefinitely in a local database.

exercises.explain: Model routing dynamically sends simple tasks to cheaper models and reserves expensive models for difficult queries to optimize costs.

cards.title: The Silent Spenders
cards.body: Have you ever feared waking up to a surprise $10,000 API bill? Without active monitoring, a simple code loop or a sudden spike in traffic can quietly spend your budget in hours.

cards.title: Cost Tracking & Alerts
cards.body: Cost monitoring involves calculating the dollar price of every LLM API call using the exact token count, while budget alerts are automated warnings triggered when total spending crosses a set limit.

cards.title: The Fuel Gauge Analogy
cards.body: Think of a budget alert like the low fuel light in your car. It doesn't shut down the engine immediately, but it warns you that you need to take action before you run out of fuel and get stranded.

cards.title: Tracking in Action
cards.body: A developer adds a metadata tag 'feature:chatbot' to their API calls. By multiplying input and output tokens by their model's rates, they see that a new prompt template accidentally doubled their daily spend from $10 to $20.

cards.title: The Passive Billing Trap
cards.body: Many believe cloud providers will automatically block excess LLM traffic for you. In reality, unless you configure explicit hard budget limits and custom alerts, providers will keep serving requests and billing you.

cards.title: Manual vs. Automated Tracking
cards.body: Compare manual billing checks with automated, real-time cost alerts.

left.text: Reviewing bills weekly; hard to pinpoint which code change caused a spike.

right.text: Immediate alerts when a code deploy starts burning cash unexpectedly.

exercises.prompt: Why is it useful to log the token cost of every individual LLM request?

options.text: It makes the model process tokens faster.
It helps identify exactly which feature or user is driving up the bill.
It automatically translates prompts into cheaper languages.
It eliminates the need for any API keys.

exercises.explain: Tracking costs per request allows you to break down expenses by user, feature, or version, helping you find the exact source of a cost spike.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You should only set a budget alert at 100% of your maximum spending limit.
exercises.explain: Budget alerts should be set to trigger at warnings (e.g., 50% or 80%) so you can fix issues before your system hits its hard limit and shuts down.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the security recommendation:
exercises.sentence: To prevent unexpected API bills from a runaway loop, we must configure budget alerts to notify developers immediately.
exercises.explain: Setting up budget alerts ensures you get notified before a sudden billing spike drains your funds.

exercises.prompt: Which of these logs represents an automated per-request cost monitoring system?

options.text: I checked the billing page on Monday and saw we spent a total of $150 last week.
We measured that our average response time is 1.5 seconds per query.
Each incoming request logs its token usage, calculating a precise cost of $0.0042 for query 9821.

exercises.explain: The third option represents automated, programmatically calculated cost logging, whereas the others are manual checks or unrelated performance benchmarks.

exercises.prompt: You deployed a new summarization pipeline and want to make sure it doesn't quietly drain your budget over the weekend due to an infinite loop. What is the best strategy to monitor and protect your system?

options.text: Track per-request cost metadata in logs and set up automated alert thresholds.
Manually check the billing dashboard once at the end of every month.
Use only the cheapest model available, even if it breaks the application features.
Block all user requests automatically if any single query takes over 2 seconds.

exercises.explain: By tracking costs on a per-request metadata level and setting up budget alerts, you can pinpoint exactly which feature (like a new summary tool) is responsible for a sudden spike before it exhausts your entire credit limit.
